title: Reflections
text: Building this ePortfolio has been one of the more practical and challenging experiences of this course. Coming in with no knowledge of web development and version control, I quickly had to get comfortable with tools like Quarto, Git, and GitHub, none of which I had used before. The process wasn’t always smooth. Small issues like line ending conflicts in Git or figuring out how to add my own projects and python code to my portfolio took more time than expected, but working through them gave me a much better understanding of how these tools actually function under the hood. What surprised me most was how much I enjoyed the design side, experimenting with structuring pages, thinking about how to present projects clearly, and making the portfolio feel like something I was genuinely proud of. It pushed me to think not just as a student submitting work, but as someone presenting themselves professionally. Overall, building this ePortfolio taught me that the technical and creative sides of data science are more connected than I initially thought. It’s not enough to do good analysis, communicating it well matters just as much. That’s a lesson I’ll carry into my career.

Coming in with no knowledge of web development and version control, I quickly had to get comfortable with tools like Quarto, Git, and GitHub, none of which I had used before. The process wasn’t always smooth. Small issues like line ending conflicts in Git or figuring out how to add my own projects and python code to my portfolio took more time than expected, but working through them gave me a much better understanding of how these tools actually function under the hood. What surprised me most was how much I enjoyed the design side, experimenting with structuring pages, thinking about how to present projects clearly, and making the portfolio feel like something I was genuinely proud of. It pushed me to think not just as a student submitting work, but as someone presenting themselves professionally. Overall, building this ePortfolio taught me that the technical and creative sides of data science are more connected than I initially thought. title: Sales Dashboard
text:
This dashboard presents a financial overview of a small-to-medium enterprise (SME), analysing monthly revenue, costs and profit across a full calendar year. Hover over any point or bar for exact values. This is an example of the dashboards we used in the Applied Consultancy Project to help provide actionalble insights. Due to data protection, the data used in the above dashbaord is mock data.


Code
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

months = ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun",
         "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec"]

revenue = [42, 58, 55, 70, 65, 80, 78, 90, 85, 95, 102, 120]
costs   = [30, 35, 33, 40, 38, 45, 44, 50, 47, 52,  55,  60]
profit  = [r - c for r, c in zip(revenue, costs)]

NAVY = "#1B2A4A"
GOLD = "#C9A84C"
RED  = "#CC3333"

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=1, cols=2,
    subplot_titles=("Revenue vs Costs (€k)", "Monthly Profit (€k)"),
    horizontal_spacing=0.12,
)

fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(x=months, y=revenue, mode="lines+markers", name="Revenue",
               line=dict(color=NAVY, width=3), marker=dict(size=8),
               hovertemplate="%{x}<br>Revenue: €%{y}k<extra></extra>"),
    row=1, col=1,
)
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(x=months, y=costs, mode="lines+markers", name="Costs",
               line=dict(color=GOLD, width=3), marker=dict(size=8),
               hovertemplate="%{x}<br>Costs: €%{y}k<extra></extra>"),
    row=1, col=1,
)

bar_colors = [NAVY if p >= 0 else RED for p in profit]
fig.add_trace(
    go.Bar(x=months, y=profit, marker_color=bar_colors, name="Profit",
           hovertemplate="%{x}<br>Profit: €%{y}k<extra></extra>"),
    row=1, col=2,
)

fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text="SME Annual Financial Overview", x=0.5,
               font=dict(size=18, color=NAVY)),
    paper_bgcolor="#F4F6FA",
    plot_bgcolor="#F4F6FA",
    height=480,
    showlegend=True,
    legend=dict(orientation="h", y=-0.15, x=0.25),
    margin=dict(l=50, r=30, t=80, b=50),
)
fig.update_yaxes(title_text="€ (thousands)", gridcolor="#d9dee8",
                 zeroline=True, zerolinecolor=GOLD)
fig.update_xaxes(gridcolor="#d9dee8")

fig.show()

title: Sales Dashboard
text:
Key Takeaways

Revenue grew steadily across the year, peaking at €120k in December
Costs remained controlled, never exceeding 50% of revenue
Profit was positive every month, reaching €60k in December

title: Projects
text:
Dublin City University | 2025–2026


Led an end-to-end data analytics consultancy engagement for an SME, focused on optimising product buying and inventory management through machine learning and data visualisation.



Served as project lead and primary point of contact, coordinating team collaboration and managing client communication throughout the project lifecycle.




Demand forecasting models — Implemented machine learning models to predict product demand and streamline supply chain operations
Interactive dashboards — Developed visualisations to translate complex datasets into clear, actionable insights for non-technical stakeholders




Python Machine Learning Data Visualisation SQL Stakeholder Communication

title: About Me
text:
Hi, I’m Alexander! I am a MSc Business Analytics candidate at Dublin City University with a background in Economics and Politics from University College Dublin.
I’m passionate about bridging the gap between complex data and real-world business strategy. My academic journey has equipped me with hands on experience in Python, SQL, and machine learning, which I’ve applied to projects like predicting demand and streamlining supply chain operations for SMEs through interactive dashboards and data-driven insights.
During my master’s dissertation, I served as project lead on an applied consultancy project, coordinating a team, delivering analytical recommendations, and building tools that translated messy datasets into clear, actionable decisions for stakeholders.
Beyond the numbers, I’ve built a solid foundation in customer-facing and leadership roles. From managing sales and client relationships at Geoghegan’s Menswear to running the social media presence of UCD’s Politics & International Relations Society.
Outside of work and study, you’ll find me on the rugby pitch with Navan RFC or representing Bhulf Tón CLG.
I’m available for opportunities from May 2026 — feel free to connect with me on LinkedIn.
You might like to look at my projects projects

title: Alexander Meehan
text:
I’m an MSc Business Analytics candidate at Dublin City University with a background in Economics and Politics. I’m interested in using data to solve real business problems, from forecasting demand to building dashboards that make complex information easy to act on.
This portfolio showcases my projects, skills and experience as I complete my masters and move into the world of data and business analytics.
I’m available for opportunities from May 2026.

title: Sales Dashboard
text:
This dashboard presents a financial overview of a small-to-medium enterprise (SME), analysing monthly revenue, costs and profit across a full calendar year. Hover over any point or bar for exact values. This is an example of the dashboards we used in the Applied Consultancy Project to help provide actionalble insights. Due to data protection, the data used in the above dashbaord is mock data.


Code
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

months = ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun",
         "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec"]

revenue = [42, 58, 55, 70, 65, 80, 78, 90, 85, 95, 102, 120]
costs   = [30, 35, 33, 40, 38, 45, 44, 50, 47, 52,  55,  60]
profit  = [r - c for r, c in zip(revenue, costs)]

NAVY = "#1B2A4A"
GOLD = "#C9A84C"
RED  = "#CC3333"

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=1, cols=2,
    subplot_titles=("Revenue vs Costs (€k)", "Monthly Profit (€k)"),
    horizontal_spacing=0.12,
)

fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(x=months, y=revenue, mode="lines+markers", name="Revenue",
               line=dict(color=NAVY, width=3), marker=dict(size=8),
               hovertemplate="%{x}<br>Revenue: €%{y}k<extra></extra>"),
    row=1, col=1,
)
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(x=months, y=costs, mode="lines+markers", name="Costs",
               line=dict(color=GOLD, width=3), marker=dict(size=8),
               hovertemplate="%{x}<br>Costs: €%{y}k<extra></extra>"),
    row=1, col=1,
)

bar_colors = [NAVY if p >= 0 else RED for p in profit]
fig.add_trace(
    go.Bar(x=months, y=profit, marker_color=bar_colors, name="Profit",
           hovertemplate="%{x}<br>Profit: €%{y}k<extra></extra>"),
    row=1, col=2,
)

fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text="SME Annual Financial Overview", x=0.5,
               font=dict(size=18, color=NAVY)),
    paper_bgcolor="#F4F6FA",
    plot_bgcolor="#F4F6FA",
    height=480,
    showlegend=True,
    legend=dict(orientation="h", y=-0.15, x=0.25),
    margin=dict(l=50, r=30, t=80, b=50),
)
fig.update_yaxes(title_text="€ (thousands)", gridcolor="#d9dee8",
                 zeroline=True, zerolinecolor=GOLD)
fig.update_xaxes(gridcolor="#d9dee8")

fig.show()
